Portfolio Project · SNP & Variant Identification

Whole-Genome Sequencing of a
Drug-Resistant E. coli Clinical Isolate

End-to-end NGS variant-calling pipeline — FastQC, Trimmomatic, BWA-MEM, FreeBayes, SnpEff — executed on the Galaxy platform, identifying antibiotic-resistance mutations in a UTI isolate.

Sample EC_UTI_2023_001
Reference NC_000913.3 · 4.64 Mb
Platform Illumina NovaSeq 6000 · 150 bp PE
Pipeline Galaxy (usegalaxy.eu)
Resistance Variants 7 / 6 genes / 5 drug classes
Pipeline QC & Trimming Alignment Variants Resistance Methods

MDR profile confirmed
01

Galaxy Workflow

The complete analysis was run on usegalaxy.eu using the importable workflow file galaxy_workflow/ecoli_variant_calling.ga. Each step is parameterised for haploid bacterial genomes.

STEP 1
FastQC v0.12
Raw QC on R1 + R2
STEP 2
Trimmomatic v0.39
Adapter removal & quality trim
STEP 2b
FastQC v0.12
Post-trim QC check
STEP 3
BWA-MEM v0.7.17
PE alignment → reference
STEP 4
SAMtools v1.18
Sort · markdup · flagstat · index
STEP 5
FreeBayes v1.3.6
Bayesian variant calling (ploidy=1)
STEP 6
VCFfilter
QUAL ≥ 200 · AF ≥ 0.90 filter
STEP 7
SnpEff v5.1d
Functional annotation (E. coli K-12)
Why FreeBayes over GATK HaplotypeCaller? FreeBayes is haplotype-based and natively supports ploidy=1 for bacterial genomes. It models the full haplotype at each locus rather than calling variants independently, which reduces false-positive calls in repetitive regions. It also requires no BQSR recalibration step, making it more suitable for organisms without a curated gold-standard variant set.
02

Quality Control & Trimming

Reads Surviving Trim
Figure 1 — Per-Base Sequence Quality. Left: raw reads show the characteristic Illumina 3′ quality decline below Q28 after position ~130 bp. Right: after Trimmomatic (SLIDINGWINDOW:4:15, MINLEN:36), the profile is uniform and consistently above Q30. Bands show the Q10–Q90 percentile range; the dashed amber line marks the Q30 threshold.
Figure 2 — Per-Sequence GC Content. The observed GC distribution (green) closely matches the theoretical curve for E. coli K-12 MG1655 (50.7% GC, blue dashed). No bimodal peaks or shoulders — no evidence of contamination or major rearrangement. ✓
# Trimmomatic PE parameters used in Galaxy ILLUMINACLIP:TruSeq3-PE.fa:2:30:10 # adapter removal LEADING:3 # drop leading bases < Q3 TRAILING:3 # drop trailing bases < Q3 SLIDINGWINDOW:4:15 # 4-base window, avg quality ≥ Q15 MINLEN:36 # discard reads shorter than 36 bp
03

Reference Alignment & Coverage

98.57%
Mapping Rate
97.21%
Properly Paired
48.7×
Mean Depth
98.94%
Genome ≥ 20×
372 bp
Mean Insert Size
Figure 3 — Genome-Wide Read Depth. Coverage is highly uniform across the 4.64 Mb chromosome (mean 48.7×, dashed cyan line). A trough near 4.17 Mb corresponds to the rRNA operons — repetitive sequences reduce unique mapping. Vertical lines mark the positions of the five key resistance genes identified in this study. The amber dotted line shows the 20× minimum threshold; 98.94% of the genome exceeds this.
Read group tagging: BWA-MEM was run with the -R flag to embed read group information (@RG ID:EC_UTI_2023_001 SM:... PL:ILLUMINA) directly in the BAM header. This is mandatory for FreeBayes and GATK variant callers, which use the read group to link reads to their sample of origin.
04

Variant Calling & Annotation

Figure 4 — Variant Allele Frequency (VAF) Profile. Left: all variants cluster above VAF 88%, consistent with a clonal bacterial population where every cell carries the same mutation. Sub-100% values reflect mapping ambiguity and PCR noise. Right: VAF vs quality score coloured by functional class. Key resistance variants (gyrA, rpoB, marR, ompC) show high QUAL (700–1000) and high VAF (>95%), maximising call confidence.
Figure 5 — SnpEff Variant Annotation Summary. Left: of 11 high-quality variants, the majority are missense (nonsynonymous) changes — direct amino-acid substitutions that alter protein function. One nonsense variant (stop-gained) in ompC truncates the porin protein. Right: gyrA carries the most nonsynonymous variants (2) — both in the quinolone-resistance-determining region (QRDR).

gyrA2,337,102C → TA90VMissense74355×98.2%

05

Antibiotic Resistance Profile

⚠ Clinical alert: This isolate displays a multidrug-resistant (MDR) genotype encompassing fluoroquinolones, rifampicin, and broad-spectrum mechanisms mediated by efflux pump upregulation and porin loss. The double gyrA mutation (D87N + A90V) predicts high-level fluoroquinolone resistance; ciprofloxacin would be clinically ineffective.
Figure 6 — Antibiotic Resistance Variant Panel. Left: predicted MIC fold-changes compared to a susceptible strain (log₂ scale). The amber dashed line marks the typical clinical resistance breakpoint (4× MIC). rpoB S531L shows the largest individual impact (256× MIC increase). Right: VAF lollipop plot showing that all resistance variants are at high allele frequency (>92%), confirming they are fixed in the clonal population — not minor sub-clonal variants.
gyrA
D87N · A90V (double QRDR)
Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin)
Both mutations in the quinolone-resistance-determining region; synergistic effect yields high-level resistance.
Predicted MIC: ≥ 32× (D87N) + 16× (A90V)
rpoB
S531L
Rifampicin
The S531L substitution is the most globally prevalent rifampicin resistance mutation; alters the rifampicin-binding pocket of RNA polymerase β-subunit.
Predicted MIC: 256×
marR
G103S
Multidrug (MarA-mediated)
Loss-of-function mutation in the MarR repressor; constitutively activates MarA, which upregulates AcrAB-TolC efflux and represses OmpF porin expression.
Predicted MIC: 8× (broad spectrum)
acrA · tolC
I355V · L457P
Multidrug (AcrAB-TolC efflux)
Structural changes in the MFP subunit (AcrA) and outer-membrane channel (TolC) may alter substrate specificity of the major MDR efflux system.
Predicted MIC: 4–6×
ompC
K163* (stop-gained)
Broad spectrum (permeability)
Nonsense mutation truncates OmpC at position 163; full loss of the outer-membrane porin reduces intracellular drug accumulation across multiple antibiotic classes.
Predicted MIC: 4× (permeability component)
06

Methods & Reproducibility

Software Versions

FastQCv0.12.1
Trimmomaticv0.39
BWA-MEMv0.7.17
SAMtoolsv1.18
BEDtoolsv2.31.0
FreeBayesv1.3.6
SnpEffv5.1d (2022-09-09)
Galaxyusegalaxy.eu (23.0)

Key Parameters

--ploidy1 (haploid bacterial genome)
--min-mapping-quality20
--min-base-quality20
--min-alternate-fraction0.05
--min-alternate-count3
VCF QUAL filter≥ 200
VCF AF filter≥ 0.90
SnpEff genome DBEscherichia_coli_k_12
Reference genome: E. coli K-12 MG1655 — NCBI RefSeq NC_000913.3 (4,641,652 bp, circular chromosome). Downloaded from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/nuccore/NC_000913.3. The Galaxy workflow file (galaxy_workflow/ecoli_variant_calling.ga) is fully importable into any Galaxy instance and reproduces this analysis end-to-end.

Resistance variant annotations were cross-referenced against the Comprehensive Antibiotic Resistance Database (CARD) and published literature on E. coli QRDR mutations (Jacoby 2005), rpoB mutation spectrum (Goldstein 2014), and the MarA/MarR regulatory circuit (Martin & Rosner 2002).

Szonja Fekete · Bioinformatics Portfolio · 2024  ·  github.com/szfekete
